answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q4: Have you noticed any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q5: Have you had any recent trauma or injury to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: I tried tylenol but it didn't help much
answers.intake_q7: Any pain with breathing or coughing?
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q9: Any history of cancer, osteoporosis, or recent steroid use?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any swelling, redness, or tenderness over the painful area?

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left side lower rib cage back pain that started 3 weeks ago. She reports tenderness over the painful area but denies fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, numbness, weakness, tingling in the legs or feet, pain with breathing or coughing, recent trauma, kidney problems, urinary symptoms, infections, cancer history, osteoporosis, or recent steroid use. She tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal pain likely due to a strain or localized inflammation of the left lower rib cage area. Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or costochondritis
2. Rib fracture or contusion (occult trauma)
3. Herpes zoster (early stage without rash)
4. Referred pain from visceral causes (e.g., renal or gastrointestinal)

P:
- Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain; max 3 grams/day (adjust for liver disease).
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated.
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen for pain and inflammation. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
 - Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution if gastrointestinal issues develop; avoid NSAIDs in patients with renal impairment.
 - Return precautions: Advise patient to seek care if she develops fever, worsening pain, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), rash, or any new urinary symptoms.
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time unless symptoms worsen or new concerning features develop.

evidence.title: Musculoskeletal Pain: What It Is, Symptoms, Treatment & Types
evidence.snippet: Chronic (long-term) musculoskeletal conditions (like lower back pain ) are also very common.

evidence.title: Musculoskeletal Chest Pain: Definition, Causes & Treatment
evidence.snippet: While it typically begins in your lower back, many people with this condition also experience inflammation in their thoracic spines and the joints in their rib cages, causing pain throughout their chest walls.
